title: K.R.C. Genk

infobox.value: Koninklijke Racing Club Genk (*Royal Racing Club Genk*)
1988; 38 years ago (1988), merge of Thor Waterschei with KFC Winterslag

(Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈkoːnɪŋkləkə ˈreːsɪŋ ˌklʏp ˈxɛŋk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")),[[a]](#cite_note-3) commonly known as **KRC Genk** or **Racing Genk**, is a Belgian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Genk](/wiki/Genk "Genk") in [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"). Racing Genk plays in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") and have won four championship titles; in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Belgian_First_Division "1998–99 Belgian First Division"), in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Belgian_First_Division "2001–02 Belgian First Division"), in [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Belgian_First_Division "2010–11 Belgian First Division") and in [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Belgian_First_Division_A "2018–19 Belgian First Division A"). They have also won five [Belgian Cups](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup"), most recently in [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Belgian_Cup "2020–21 Belgian Cup"). They qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") group stage in the [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_UEFA_Champions_League "2002–03 UEFA Champions League"), [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_UEFA_Champions_League "2011–12 UEFA Champions League") and [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League "2019–20 UEFA Champions League").

was founded in [1923](/wiki/1923_in_football_(soccer) "1923 in football (soccer)") and that gave it the matricule number 322. On its 35th anniversary the club added the Royal prefix *[Koninklijke](/wiki/Koninklijke "Koninklijke")* to their name to become KFC Winterslag. In [1972–73](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_in_Belgian_football "1972–73 in Belgian football") Winterslag reached the [second division](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division") and they eventually qualified for the [1974–75 Belgian First Division](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Belgian_First_Division "1974–75 Belgian First Division") after finishing second in the [second division final round](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division_final_round "Belgian Second Division final round"). They had taken advantage of the increase in the number of first division clubs (from 16 to 20). The club ended the season in last place but won the second division right after.

The new club was named KRC Genk and as it kept the Winterslag ranking, it began in the first division but finished last. The next year Genk won the final round in 2nd division and then played 4 seasons in the [first division](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division "Belgian First Division"). In 1995 the club hired [Aimé Anthuenis](/wiki/Aim%C3%A9_Anthuenis "Aimé Anthuenis") a coach and Racing finished second and skipped the final round as two first division teams merged ([Seraing](/wiki/R.F.C._Seraing_(1904) "R.F.C. Seraing (1904)") and Standard Liège). After an eighth place in 1997, the club had a good 1997–98 season with a cup win and a second place in the championship. In its first European season, Racing Genk eliminated successively [Apolonia Fier](/wiki/KF_Apolonia_Fier "KF Apolonia Fier") and [MSV Duisburg](/wiki/MSV_Duisburg "MSV Duisburg") but it lost to [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca") in the round of 16 after two draws (1–1 on aggregate) in the last [Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"). The season was ended well as Genk won its first Belgian championship in May, with manager Aimé Anthuenis then moving to [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht").

The 2010–11 season started well for KRC Genk when they beat [Inter Turku](/wiki/FC_Inter_Turku "FC Inter Turku") with 1–5 in Finland. They progressed to the 4th qualifying round of the Europa League and drew the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") club [Porto](/wiki/FC_Porto "FC Porto"). Genk lost both games against Porto, despite two good performances.

**Koninklijke Racing Club Genk** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈkoːnɪŋkləkə ˈreːsɪŋ ˌklʏp ˈxɛŋk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")),[[a]](#cite_note-3) commonly known as **KRC Genk** or **Racing Genk**, is a Belgian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Genk](/wiki/Genk "Genk") in [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"). Racing Genk plays in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") and have won four championship titles; in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Belgian_First_Division "1998–99 Belgian First Division"), in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Belgian_First_Division "2001–02 Belgian First Division"), in [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Belgian_First_Division "2010–11 Belgian First Division") and in [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Belgian_First_Division_A "2018–19 Belgian First Division A"). They have also won five [Belgian Cups](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup"), most recently in [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Belgian_Cup "2020–21 Belgian Cup"). They qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") group stage in the [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_UEFA_Champions_League "2002–03 UEFA Champions League"), [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_UEFA_Champions_League "2011–12 UEFA Champions League") and [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League "2019–20 UEFA Champions League").

The club formed in [1988](/wiki/1988_in_football_(soccer) "1988 in football (soccer)") by the merger of [Waterschei Thor](/wiki/K._Waterschei_S.V._Thor_Genk "K. Waterschei S.V. Thor Genk") with **KFC Winterslag**, from which it took over the [matricule number](/wiki/Matricule "Matricule"). It has been one of the most successful clubs in Belgium since the late 1990s and so they regularly qualify for European competitions. The club has been playing in the first division since the [1996–97 season](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Belgian_First_Division "1996–97 Belgian First Division"). They play their home matches in the [Cegeka Arena](/wiki/Cegeka_Arena "Cegeka Arena"). Their main outfit is blue and white.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### KFC Winterslag history (1923–1988)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: KFC Winterslag history (1923–1988)")]

The club **FC Winterslag** was founded in [1923](/wiki/1923_in_football_(soccer) "1923 in football (soccer)") and that gave it the matricule number 322. On its 35th anniversary the club added the Royal prefix *[Koninklijke](/wiki/Koninklijke "Koninklijke")* to their name to become KFC Winterslag. In [1972–73](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_in_Belgian_football "1972–73 in Belgian football") Winterslag reached the [second division](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division") and they eventually qualified for the [1974–75 Belgian First Division](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Belgian_First_Division "1974–75 Belgian First Division") after finishing second in the [second division final round](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division_final_round "Belgian Second Division final round"). They had taken advantage of the increase in the number of first division clubs (from 16 to 20). The club ended the season in last place but won the second division right after.

KFC Winterslag reached the 5th place in [1981](/wiki/1981_in_football_(soccer) "1981 in football (soccer)") which allowed them to play UEFA Cup matches, where it defeated [Bryne FK](/wiki/Bryne_FK "Bryne FK") from Norway and English Premier league giant [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.").[[3]](#cite_note-4) In the next round it was knocked out by [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United "Dundee United") from Scotland. Two seasons later it was relegated to the second stage after a disappointing last place. That season [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") won the championship on bribery in a match against the club of Waterschei Thor that would eventually merge with the matricule number 322. Following a spell of four seasons in the second division, Winterslag found its place again in the [first division](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division "Belgian First Division") by winning the [1987](/wiki/1987_in_football_(soccer) "1987 in football (soccer)") final round, one point ahead of [Tongeren](/wiki/K.S.K._Tongeren "K.S.K. Tongeren"). It finished 15th on 18 but at the end of the season, the club merged with the neighbour club of Waterschei Thor which was playing in the second division since its relegation in [1986](/wiki/1986_in_football_(soccer) "1986 in football (soccer)").

### K Waterschei SV Thor Genk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: K Waterschei SV Thor Genk")]

Main article: [K. Waterschei S.V. Thor Genk](/wiki/K._Waterschei_S.V._Thor_Genk "K. Waterschei S.V. Thor Genk")

K Waterschei SV Thor was created in [1919](/wiki/1919_in_football_(soccer) "1919 in football (soccer)") as Waterschei's Sport Vereeniging Thor with Thor being the acronym of *Tot Herstel Onzer Rechten* (English: To recover our rights). It received matricule number n°533. The club enjoyed a spell in the first division in the late 1950s to the early 1960s and again from 1978 to 1986.

During the 1982–83 season, the match between [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") and Waterschei [had been fixed](/w/index.php?title=Had_been_fixed&action=edit&redlink=1 "Had been fixed (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Affaire_Standard-Waterschei "fr:Affaire Standard-Waterschei")] and Standard eventually won the championship. Waterschei won the Belgian Cup twice (1980 and 1982). The latter victory led to them reaching the semi-finals of the [European Cup Winners Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup") in the 1982–83 season. After defeating [PSG](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.") in the quarter-finals, Waterschei lost the first leg of the semi-final 5–1 at [Pittodrie Stadium](/wiki/Pittodrie_Stadium "Pittodrie Stadium"), home of the eventual winners, [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen_F.C. "Aberdeen F.C.") A 1–0 victory in Waterschei, courtesy of Eddy Voordeckers, could not reverse the position.

After two seasons in the second division, K. Waterschei SV Thor Genk merged with KFC Winterslag in 1988 to form the current KRC Genk.

### KRC Genk (1988–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: KRC Genk (1988–present)")]

**1990s and 2000s**

The new club was named KRC Genk and as it kept the Winterslag ranking, it began in the first division but finished last. The next year Genk won the final round in 2nd division and then played 4 seasons in the [first division](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division "Belgian First Division"). In 1995 the club hired [Aimé Anthuenis](/wiki/Aim%C3%A9_Anthuenis "Aimé Anthuenis") a coach and Racing finished second and skipped the final round as two first division teams merged ([Seraing](/wiki/R.F.C._Seraing_(1904) "R.F.C. Seraing (1904)") and Standard Liège). After an eighth place in 1997, the club had a good 1997–98 season with a cup win and a second place in the championship. In its first European season, Racing Genk eliminated successively [Apolonia Fier](/wiki/KF_Apolonia_Fier "KF Apolonia Fier") and [MSV Duisburg](/wiki/MSV_Duisburg "MSV Duisburg") but it lost to [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca") in the round of 16 after two draws (1–1 on aggregate) in the last [Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"). The season was ended well as Genk won its first Belgian championship in May, with manager Aimé Anthuenis then moving to [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht").

Genk played in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") in 1999–2000 but lost in the second qualifying round to [Maribor](/wiki/NK_Maribor "NK Maribor"). The season was salvaged by winning the Belgian Cup again, this time to Standard, but Genk ended the championship in 9th place. It finished 11th in the following season and lost in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") second round to [Werder Bremen](/wiki/SV_Werder_Bremen "SV Werder Bremen") after a win against [FC Zürich](/wiki/FC_Z%C3%BCrich "FC Zürich"). After this poor spell, Genk won the championship once more in the 2001–02 season. In 2002–03, they reached the Champions League group stages for the first time in their history. Although they came 4th, they impressed fans with draws against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F."), [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") and [AEK Athens](/wiki/AEK_Athens_F.C. "AEK Athens F.C.").

In the 2006–07 season, Genk finished second to Anderlecht. The Limburgians had been ahead almost the entire season but were pipped at the post by Anderlecht after losing at Germinal Beerschot. The 2007–08 season was a disaster, as Genk failed to finish in the top half of the division, ending in a disappointing tenth place.

Three bad seasons followed. Genk finished the 2007–08 season on 45 points and in 10th spot in the league: their worst result in seven years. The 2008–09 season was poor for Genk as well, finishing 8th in the league. The season ended on a positive note with them winning the [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup"), which gave them a ticket to the fourth Europa League qualifying round. The 2009–10 season started badly when they were knocked out of the Europa League by [Lille](/wiki/Lille_OSC "Lille OSC"). Things did not go well in the domestic league either. Manager Hein Vanhaezebrouck was fired in December and was replaced by [Franky Vercauteren](/wiki/Franky_Vercauteren "Franky Vercauteren"). Genk finished 11th, but Vercauteren led the club to European football by beating derby rival [Sint-Truiden](/wiki/Sint-Truidense_V.V. "Sint-Truidense V.V.") in the final of Play-offs II.

**2010s**

The 2010–11 season started well for KRC Genk when they beat [Inter Turku](/wiki/FC_Inter_Turku "FC Inter Turku") with 1–5 in Finland. They progressed to the 4th qualifying round of the Europa League and drew the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") club [Porto](/wiki/FC_Porto "FC Porto"). Genk lost both games against Porto, despite two good performances.

On 30 January 2010, KRC Genk announced that coach Franky Vercauteren signed a new contract that ran untl June 2013.

They only lost their first game of the season on the 20th matchday and started the Play-offs in second place. The club won the [2010–11 Belgian Pro League](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Belgian_Pro_League "2010–11 Belgian Pro League") after drawing 1–1 with title challengers [Standard Liége](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A9ge "Standard Liége").[[4]](#cite_note-5)
This was KRC Genk's third league win in its history and its supporters celebrated with a pitch invasion straight after the final whistle.

On 11 August, coach Frank Vercauteren confirmed he was leaving Genk and signed with [United Arab Emirate](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") club [Al-Jazira](/wiki/Al_Jazira_Club "Al Jazira Club"). In the 3rd Qualifying Round of the 2011–12 UEFA Champions League KRC Genk beat [FK Partizan](/wiki/FK_Partizan "FK Partizan") over two legs and drew [Maccabi Haifa](/wiki/Maccabi_Haifa_F.C. "Maccabi Haifa F.C.") in the play-off Round. Maccabi Haifa beat Genk 2–1 in the first leg in Israel, while the second leg was won by Genk with the same 2–1 score in Belgium. During the penalty shoot-out, goalkeeper [László Köteles](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_K%C3%B6teles "László Köteles") helped Genk to qualify by saving two penalties.[[5]](#cite_note-6) For the second time in its history, KRC Genk reached the group stages of the UEFA Champions League. They were drawn in Group E with [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF") and [Bayer Leverkusen](/wiki/Bayer_04_Leverkusen "Bayer 04 Leverkusen").

In late August 2011, [Mario Been](/wiki/Mario_Been "Mario Been") was announced as the new manager.
The Champions League campaign was one with ups and downs. Genk got a 1–1 result against both Chelsea and Bayer Leverkusen and a goalless draw against Valencia. Away from home, Genk lost all three games.
The season in the Jupiler League was a difficult one, with Genk only just qualifying for the play-offs by finishing sixth in the regular competition. In the play-offs however, Genk started to play better and climbed up to third place. By finishing in third place, KRC Genk qualified for the third qualifying round of the [Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League").

The 2012–13 season started well for Genk by qualifying for the Europa League group stage after beating [Aktobe](/wiki/FC_Aktobe "FC Aktobe") and [FC Luzern](/wiki/FC_Luzern "FC Luzern"). In this group stage KRC Genk performed very well and ultimately won the group without a single defeat. Genk finished first with three points more than [Basel](/wiki/FC_Basel "FC Basel") and by doing so, qualified for the next round where they faced [VfB Stuttgart](/wiki/VfB_Stuttgart "VfB Stuttgart"). It was the first time in the club's history that they played European football after the winter break.
Stuttgart got the better of Genk over the two games.
In the league, Genk qualified for the play-offs and performed well until the title was out of reach; fifth place was the result. Genk ended their season on a positive note by winning the Belgian Cup. They defeated [Cercle Brugge](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V.") in the final, in front of 30,000 Genk fans.

In the 2016–2017 season, Genk participated in the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_UEFA_Europa_League "2016–17 UEFA Europa League"); they started playing in the second qualifying round and [qualified](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_UEFA_Europa_League_qualifying_phase_and_play-off_round "2016–17 UEFA Europa League qualifying phase and play-off round") for the third qualifying round (on 21 July 2016)[[6]](#cite_note-Buducnost2-7) and the play-off round (on 4 August 2016).[[7]](#cite_note-Cork2-8) They won their group with 3 home victories over [Athletic Bilbao](/wiki/Athletic_Bilbao "Athletic Bilbao"), [Rapid Wien](/wiki/Rapid_Wien "Rapid Wien") and [Sassuolo](/wiki/U.S._Sassuolo_Calcio "U.S. Sassuolo Calcio") and after defeating [Astra Giurgiu](/wiki/FC_Astra_Giurgiu "FC Astra Giurgiu") (2–2 and 1–0) in the 2nd round they drew [KAA Gent](/wiki/KAA_Gent "KAA Gent") from the domestic [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") with 2 confusing games Gent-Genk, Genk-Gent and an impressive 2–5 away victory. Even when Genk got beaten in the quarter final against [Celta de Vigo](/wiki/Celta_de_Vigo "Celta de Vigo") (3–2 and 1–1), it was Genk's most successful European season.

Genk won the [2018–19 Belgian First Division A](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Belgian_First_Division_A "2018–19 Belgian First Division A") for the fourth time in their history, hence they qualified for the [2019–20 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League "2019–20 UEFA Champions League") after an eight-year absence.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Genk started their [2019–20 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League "2019–20 UEFA Champions League") campaign with a poor 6–2 loss against Austrian club [Red Bull Salzburg](/wiki/FC_Red_Bull_Salzburg "FC Red Bull Salzburg"). In the second match they drew 0–0 against Napoli, and in the third match they lost 1–4 against Liverpool.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stadium")]

Main article: [Cegeka Arena](/wiki/Cegeka_Arena "Cegeka Arena")

Prior to the merger of the clubs in 1988, KFC Winterslag played at De Noordlaan, whilst Thor Waterschei played at the Andre Dumont Stadium.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10) The Andre Dumont Stadium was renovated at significant cost following the merger and became known as the [Thyl Geyselinckstadion](/wiki/Cegeka_Arena "Cegeka Arena") after Thyl Geyselinck, who oversaw the merger of the clubs, and it was where KRC Genk played their matches from 1990.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) De Noordlaan was then used as a ground for the reserve and youth teams of KRC Genk before falling into disrepair around the year 2000 and being demolished in 2004 to make way for housing.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10) The Thyl Geyselinckstadion was redeveloped in 1999 to hold 24,500 spectators and was renamed to the Fenixstadion.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Following a sponsorship deal with IT company [Cegeka](/wiki/Cegeka "Cegeka") in 2021, the stadium was renamed to *Cegeka Arena,* having previously been known as the *Luminus Arena* due to a similar sponsorship deal with [Luminus](/wiki/Luminus "Luminus").[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## Youth academy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KRC_Genk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Youth academy")]

Genk is well known for its outstanding youth academy. In 2003 they built their youth center next to their stadium and set up a youth program with Ronny Vangeneugden. There are further plans to build a boarding school and some synthetic pitches. In the past and now, many young players have found their way through the youth system. Some examples are [Yannick Carrasco](/wiki/Yannick_Carrasco "Yannick Carrasco"), [Jelle Vossen](/wiki/Jelle_Vossen "Jelle Vossen"), [Dennis Praet](/wiki/Dennis_Praet "Dennis Praet"), [Steven Defour](/wiki/Steven_Defour "Steven Defour"), [Christian Benteke](/wiki/Christian_Benteke "Christian Benteke"), [Thibaut Courtois](/wiki/Thibaut_Courtois "Thibaut Courtois"), [Divock Origi](/wiki/Divock_Origi "Divock Origi"), [Timothy Castagne](/wiki/Timothy_Castagne "Timothy Castagne"), [Leandro Trossard](/wiki/Leandro_Trossard "Leandro Trossard") and [Kevin De Bruyne](/wiki/Kevin_De_Bruyne "Kevin De Bruyne").

Their scouting is also highly regarded. Players such as [Kalidou Koulibaly](/wiki/Kalidou_Koulibaly "Kalidou Koulibaly"), [Wilfred Ndidi](/wiki/Wilfred_Ndidi "Wilfred Ndidi"), [Leon Bailey](/wiki/Leon_Bailey "Leon Bailey"), [Sergej Milinković-Savić](/wiki/Sergej_Milinkovi%C4%87-Savi%C4%87 "Sergej Milinković-Savić"), [Daniel Muñoz](/wiki/Daniel_Mu%C3%B1oz_(footballer) "Daniel Muñoz (footballer)") and [Sander Berge](/wiki/Sander_Berge "Sander Berge") all played for Genk.
